coredata.dc:title: Reversible detection of Hg(II) in pure water based on thymine modified nitrogen, sulfur co-doped carbon dots combined with antidote
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Min
Wang, Jun
Zhang, Qianbo
Zhang, Jinsheng
Chen, Zhiming
Sun, Rongguo
coredata.dc:description: Conventional Hg2+ visual sensors are unsustainable, hindering their practical application for improved water quality and health. In order to address this challenge, herein, N, S co-doped carbon nanodots (NS-CDs) were prepared and well characterized, presented the fluorescent monitoring for Hg2+ over other metal ions with the limit of detection (LOD) of 0.47 µM. Next, the CDs were successfully modified by thymine without any fluorescence labelling (referred to as T-NS-CDs). The sensitivity to Hg2+ cloud be noticeable enhanced due to the formation of T-Hg2+-T specific base pairs. Accordingly, the LOD was calculated with values as low as 1.56 nM. Furthermore, Hg2+ could be released and complexed with antidote (meso-2,3-dimercaptosuccinic acid) (DMSA-Hg2+), being the responsible for the reversible interconversion between T-Hg2+-T and DMSA-Hg2+. Interestingly, the proposed sensing system also applies to the fluorescent sensing for Hg2+ in tap water with satisfactory recoveries (96.97 %–101.38 %, RSD < 2 %). Thus, by simply combination of elemental doping and surface functionalization, the surface state and functionalities of CDs could be tailorable, endowing the fluorometric sensing towards Hg2+ in environmental system.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Mercury ion
Carbon nanodots
Surface modification
Thymine

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Optical properties of NS-CDs and the visual detection for Hg2+
The fluorescent detection for Hg2+ by T-NS-CDs
Versatility study of T-NS-CDs for detection of multiple heavy metal ions

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) FT-IR spectra of NS-CDs; (b) XPS survey spectrum of NS-CDs, and high-resolution spectra for (c) C 1s, (d) N 1s, (e) O 1s, and (f) S 2p.

(a) Fluorescence responses of NS-CDs upon addition of Hg2+ with various concentrations from 0 to 18 μM (λex = 395 nm); (b) Stern-Volmer plots of NS-CDs for Hg2+. Inset: fluorescence linear plot of NS-CDs against Hg2+; (c) Relationship between the (F – F0)/F0 and Hg2+ concentration. Inset: the calculation of LOD.
(a) UV–vis absorbance of NS-CDs (0.54 × 106 µg/L), T-NS-CDs (0.5 × 106 µg/L) and thymine (2 × 10-5 M) in HEPES buffer solution; (b) FT-IR spectrum of T-Hg(II)-T and T-NS-CDs.
(a) XPS survey spectrum, and high-resolution spectra for (b) C 1s, (c) N 1s, (d) O 1s, and (e) S 2p, respectively.
(a) TEM image; (b) particle size distribution obtained from TEM image; (c) XRD patterns of the T-NS-CDs.

(a) Fluorescence responses of T-NS-CDs upon addition of Hg2+ with various concentrations from 0 to 5.1 μM (Inset: the responding pictures before and after addition of Hg2+); (b) Stern-Volmer plots of T-NS-CDs for Hg2+, Inset: fluorescence linear plot of T-NS-CDs against Hg2+; (c) Relationship between the (F – F0)/F0 and Hg2+ concentration. Inset: the calculation of LOD.
Synthesis procedures for T-NS-CDs and reversible detection of Hg2+.
The proposed interconversion between dispersoids and aggregates of T-NS-CDs in the absence (left)/presence (right) of Hg2+.
A comparison between the present method and the previous developed nano carbon-based analytical probes for the determination of Hg2+.
Analytical results for the detection of Hg2+ ions in real water samples (n = 3).

head.ce:title: Reversible detection of Hg(II) in pure water based on thymine modified nitrogen, sulfur co-doped carbon dots combined with antidote

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Materials Science, Guizhou Normal University, Guiyang 550025, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Materials Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Materials Science, Guizhou Normal University, Guiyang 550025, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Min Chen and Jun Wang contributed equally.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Reversible detection of Hg(II) in pure water was achieved based on thymine modified N, S co-doped carbon dots combined with antidote (DMSA).

Conventional Hg2+ visual sensors are unsustainable, hindering their practical application for improved water quality and health. In order to address this challenge, herein, N, S co-doped carbon nanodots (NS-CDs) were prepared and well characterized, presented the fluorescent monitoring for Hg2+ over other metal ions with the limit of detection (LOD) of 0.47 µM. Next, the CDs were successfully modified by thymine without any fluorescence labelling (referred to as T-NS-CDs). The sensitivity to Hg2+ cloud be noticeable enhanced due to the formation of T-Hg2+-T specific base pairs. Accordingly, the LOD was calculated with values as low as 1.56 nM. Furthermore, Hg2+ could be released and complexed with antidote (meso-2,3-dimercaptosuccinic acid) (DMSA-Hg2+), being the responsible for the reversible interconversion between T-Hg2+-T and DMSA-Hg2+. Interestingly, the proposed sensing system also applies to the fluorescent sensing for Hg2+ in tap water with satisfactory recoveries (96.97 %–101.38 %, RSD < 2 %). Thus, by simply combination of elemental doping and surface functionalization, the surface state and functionalities of CDs could be tailorable, endowing the fluorometric sensing towards Hg2+ in environmental system.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: With continuous development of modern industry, environmental pollution caused by heavy metal ions has been considered as one of the severest problems worldwide, giving rise to the immense concern [1]. Non-biodegradable mercuric ion (Hg2+) is commonly deemed to be one of the most toxic water pollutants. It has been well documented that even at very low level of Hg2+ in the body could produce serious a variety of diseases such as cardiovascular disease, minamata disease, developmental delay, cancer etc [2]. Therefore, the methods for visual sensing of Hg2+ with high selectivity and sensitivity have been triggering increasing interests [3–6].

Apart from the traditional instrumental methods, a large number of fluorometric sensors were well designed based on organic dyes [1,7–9]. However, those visual sensors always suffer from disadvantages such as water-insolubility, biological incompatibility, tediously synthetic protocols, and so on. Besides, fluorescent sensors based on hydrophobic semiconductor quantum dots were also reported. Those sensors produce toxicity and environmental pollution due to the release of heavy metal ions during the detection process [10]. Otherwise, they possess the shortcoming of aggregation-induced fluorescence quenching in aqueous solution [11]. Compared with those sensors, fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) are emerging as sustainable candidate ones for various analytes in environment owing to their tunable photoluminescence, high sensitivity, low toxicity and high compatibility, low-cost sources, as well as easy synthesis and surface modification [12–15].

Owing to relatively poor sensitivity of pristine CDs for heavy metal ions, the surface state and structural characteristics of CDs always be well designed to improve sensing performance [3,14]. For Hg2+ sensing, the following two main strategies were adopted. One is elemental doping with N or / and S to form N/N,S-CDs [16–18]. This approach endows additional desirable functionalities (–NH2, -SH and -CS) on obtained CDs and enables the sensitive detection of Hg2+ due to the strong soft–soft interactions. For instance, citric acid (CA), urea and thiourea derived N,S-CDs were shown to detect Hg2+ with LOD of 62 nM [17]. Also, Z. Sun et al. prepared N, S codoped CDs from citric acid and thiourea using hydrothermal method at 160 °C for the detection of Cu2+ and Fe3+, while sensing performance for Hg2+ was not mentioned [19]. In fact, the sensitivity of those N/N, S-CDs was not satisfactory [3,20]. In addition, surface functionalization is the other acceptable strategy [13,21–25], in which the specific affinity between surface functionalities of CDs with Hg2+ is responsible for the high selectivity and sensitivity. The mushroom-based pristine CDs displayed a LOD of 4.13 nM for Hg2+, after surface functionalization by dihydrolipoic acid, the sensitivity was dramatic improved with LOD of 17.4 pM [22].

As we known, thymine (T) is one of the nucleic acid base of aptamers and oligonucleotides (DNA or RNA), which can bind to Hg2+ and form T-Hg2+-T specific base pairs with high affinity [26]. The specific interaction has been employed in Hg2+ sensor design based on fluorescence dyes [27,28]. Besides, nucleic acid functionalized CDs for Hg2+ detection also have been reported [29,30]. Note that the nucleic acid always should be well designed, synthesized and be labeled by fluorophore. However, to the best of our knowledge, there is no reported thymine-modified CDs through hydrogen interaction for specific responses to Hg2+. Therefore, the principal objective of the present study consists in the well development of N, S co-doped and functionalized CDs without fluorescent labels, thereby improving the sensitivity for Hg2+ sensing.

Inspired by these challenges, and in view of our research interests in visual sensing [31–33], herein, we prepared N, S co-doped NS-CDs using CA and thiourea as carbon and sulfur source, respectively. NS-CDs displayed the fluorescent monitoring for Hg2+ over other metal ions. And then, to improve the sensitivity for Hg2+, the T-NS-CDs modified by thymine were obtained with the high sensitivity towards Hg2+ because of the formation of T-Hg2+-T specific base pairs, leading to the fluorescent “turn OFF”. Next, after addition of chelating agent DMSA, T-Hg2+-T specific base pairs were dissociated and Hg2+ would be released and further formed the DMSA-Hg2+ complex [34], rendering the fluorescence “turn ON”. Consequently, reversible sensing platform of Hg2+ with the fluorescence “ON–OFF–ON” could be easy to establish based on the competition coordination between T-Hg2+-T and DMSA-Hg2+, Scheme 1 . Most notably, the proposed approach has been applied to the fluorescent sensing for Hg2+ in tap water with satisfactory recoveries (96.97 %–101.38 %).

All chemicals were commercially available, and all experiments were performed at 25 ℃ and in double deionized water, unless noted otherwise. Citric acid, thiourea, thymine, meso-2,3-dimercaptosuccinic acid (DMSA) and 4-(2-hydroxyethyl)-1-piperazineethanesulfonic acid (HEPES) were obtained from Aladdin (Shanghai, China). Analytical reagents (mercury dichloride, sodium chloride, potassium chloride, magnesium chloride, calcium chloride, copper(II) chloride dihydrate, cadmium chloride, iron(III) chloride hexahydrate, cobaltous chloride, nickel chloride, chromium chloride hexahydrate, zinc sulfate heptahydrate, manganese(II) sulfate monohydrate and lead nitrate were used as cations (Hg2+, Na+, K+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Cu2+, Cd2+, Fe3+, Co2+, Ni2+, Cr3+, Zn2+, Mn2+, Pb2+) source in other experiments, respectively.

Transmission electron microscope (TEM) images were obtained by FEI Tecnai G2 F20 S-Twin 200 kV (FEI, USA). X-Ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) were acquired on a Thermo Scientific K-Alpha (Manchester, U.K.) with an Al-Kα X-ray (1486.6 eV) excitation source running at 12 kV. X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were obtained from a XD-2X/M4600 X-ray diffract meter with Cu Ka radiation (40 kV, 20 mA, λ = 1.5405 Å). Fourier transform infrared spectra (FT-IR) were recorded on a Thermo Scientific Nicolet 6700 FT-IR spectrometer (Sugar Land, TX, USA) with the KBr pellet technique in the range of 400–4000 cm−1. Shimadzu UV-2550 and FL970 fluorescence spectrometer (Techcomp, China) were used to obtain UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra using quartz cells of 1.0 cm path length, respectively. The required pH was measured by means of a PHS-3C pH meter and controlled by HEPES (pH = 7.04, 10 mM). By the way, all of fluorescent spectra were recorded with the excitation wavelength of 395 nm by 5 nm spit.

NS-CDs were prepared according to the previous reported [14,29] one-pot hydrothermal method employing citric acid and thiourea as carbon and sulfur source. Firstly, the optimum of the best molar ratio of carbon and sulfur source has been viewed. The detailed preparation procedure is described as follows: 1.92 g of citric acid and 2.28 g of thiourea were dissolved in 30 mL methanol. After ultrasonication for 30 min, the mixture was put into a 50 mL Teflon-lined autoclave, heated at 180 °C for 10 h, and allowed to cool to room temperature in air. The product was dissolved with water, and re-purified with a 0.22 μM filter membrane to remove any solid residue. The filtrates were further purified using dialysis bag (1000 Da, molecular weight cut-off) for 24 h. It should be noticed that the double deionized water would be replaced every 4 h. Finally, the dialysis fluids were concentrated in rotary evaporator, and a blank powder product was obtained by drying at 60 °C.

The detailed synthesis of T-functionalized T-NS-CDs is described as follows: NS-CDs (0.1 g) was added to 10 mL of 0.1 M NaOH solution and refluxed for 1 h. Then, the solution of thymine (0.3 g) in 3 mL DMSO was added, and the mixture was continued to reflux for another 1 h. Finally, the solution was washed with ethyl acetate to remove excess thymine and purified by distilled water using a dialysis membrane (1000 Da, molecular weight cut-off) for 24 h. After concentration of dialysis fluids, black T-NS-CDs were obtained. Special notes that the detailed molecular information of raw materials involved in the preparation experiments is listed in table S1.

For the fluorescent titration, 2 mL NS-CDs (2.17 × 104 µg/L)/T-NS-CDs (2.5 × 104 µg/L) solution was taken and the titration was done with different metal ions (250 μM) in HEPES buffer solution (pH = 7.04, 10 mM). The changes in intensity were monitored at maximum emission wavelength. The titration curve was obtained by plotting the intensity at maximum emission wavelength versus the concentration of metal ions. The fluorescence quenching coefficient (KSV) and the limit of detection (LOD) were obtained from the Stern-Volmer equation [35] and 3σ/S method [36] (See supporting information for details).

To estimate the accuracy of proposed method in tap water, recovery studies were executed at different concentrations levels of Hg2+ (50, 70, 90 and 110 µM). The recovery percentage could be determined from the following equation:
R e c o v e r y
( 100 % )
=
C
exp
C
add
× 100
where Cexp represents the average concentration of the Hg2+ determined experimentally in the fortified samples, while Cadd represents the concentration of added Hg2+ in the non-fortified sample, respectively.

As mentioned above, citric acid and thiourea were employed as carbon and sulfur source to prepare NS-CDs. The best molar ratio of them was investigated firstly based on the luminescence performance of NS-CDs in the absence/presence of Hg2+. The synthetic temperature and time were fixed at 180 ℃ and 10 h, respectively. When the molar ratio was changed in the range of 1:1 to 1:10 (table S2), the differentiable fluorescence quenching was observed upon addition of same amount of Hg2+, Fig. S1. And the quenching effects first increased and then decreased, reaching the maximum at molar ratio of 1:3. Therefore, the optimal molar ratio of 1:3 was adopted for the preparation of NS-CDs.

With NS-CDs in hand, the surface functional groups and chemical composition were firstly studied. It is desirable that N, S co-doped CDs could be formed to promote the fluorescence sensing performance of Hg2+. In FT-IR spectrum (Fig. 1
a), the broad absorption band centered at 3424 cm−1 is attributed to stretching vibrations of –OH and –NH [18]. While the characteristic absorption bands at 1697, 1606 and 1128 cm−1 are stemmed from of CO, CC and C-O bonds [18,37], respectively. And the peaks at 1568 cm−1 and 1189 cm−1 are assigned to the stretching vibration modes of CS and C-S [5,19]. Then, the elemental composition and surface bonding states of NS-CDs were surveyed by XPS spectra. It can be confirmed from Fig. 1 b that NS-CDs are made up of carbon (63.75 %), oxygen (25.02 %), nitrogen (10.55 %) and surfer (0.66 %). In the high resolution C1s XPS spectrum (Fig. 1 c), the fitted three peaks at 284.37 eV, 285.81 eV, and 287.82 eV are attributed to C–C/CC, C–O (N, S) and O–CO/CS/CN [19], respectively. The deconvolution analysis of N1s profile suggests the presence of C-N at 397.49 eV and pyrrolic N at 399.56 eV [18,37], as shown in Fig. 1 d. Moreover, the peaks at 531.36 eV and 533.49 eV in O1s spectrum (Fig. 1 e) correspond to CO and C–O/OH bonds [38]. Besides, important to mention that the peaks located at 164.09, 164.39, 167.03 and 168.33 eV might be belonged to C-Sn-C (n1 or 2), -CS-, and -C–SO x –C (x = 2, 167.03 eV; x = 3, 168.33 eV) [5], respectively. These observations from FT-IR and XPS analysis explicate the coexistence of N, S on the surface of CDs, guaranteeing the work well for Hg2+ sensing.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties of NS-CDs and the visual detection for Hg2+
ce:section.ce:para:
The photophysical properties of NS-CDs were firstly studied using UV–vis absorption and fluorescent spectra in HEPES buffer solution (pH = 7.04, 10 mM), Fig. S2 . The UV–vis profile displays two absorption bands centered at about 203 nm and 334 nm (Fig. S2
a), which could be attributed to the π-π* transition of aromatic sp2 domain and the n-π* transition of CO/CS bond, consistence with the results of FT-IR and XPS analysis. Correspondingly, the excitation and emission spectra (Fig. S2 b) are obtained with maximum excitation wavelength of 395 nm and emission wavelength of 459 nm, reflecting the strong blue emission under irradiation at 365 nm (inset of Fig. S2 b).

Upon additions of equimolar amounts (250 μM) of different metal ions into NS-CDs solution, the most distinguishing fluorescence quenching was identified in the presence of Hg2+ ( Fig. S3 ). It is necessary to motion, Cu2+, Fe3+ and other metal cations triggered decreases of fluorescence intensity were almost negligible. As shown in Fig. 2 a, with the increase of Hg2+ (0–18 μM), the fluorescence intensity decreases gradually and quenches nearly completely. The KSV is found out to be 3.25 × 105 M−1 with the correlation coefficient of 0.99 (Fig. 2 b). Furthermore, a satisfactory linear relationship (y = 0.23 + 1.31 × 107 x) could be fitted between the fluorescence quenching degree (F0 − F)/F0 at 459 nm and the Hg2+ concentration, and the LOD also is calculated as 0.47 µM, (Fig. 2). Regrettably, NS-CDs do display a better selectivity but indifferent sensitivity for Hg2+, thereby preventing their use in real aqueous environments.

To enhancing the sensitivity towards Hg2+, as-prepared CDs were modified by thymine based on hydrogen interaction, which are referred to as T-NS-CDs. In comparison with the evidential spectral changes, we carried out the UV–vis and IR experiments. Evidently, in UV–vis profile of T-NS-CDs (Fig. 3
a), the characteristic absorption of thymine at 269 nm appears along with the negligible redshifts to 211 and 335 nm of π-π* and n-π* transition absorptions. Meanwhile, the main intrinsic absorption bands of thymine are covered in its IR spectrum compared with that of NS-CDs and T-Hg2+-T. Specifically, as can be seen from Fig. 3 b, they are 3440 cm−1 for N–H/O-H, 3062 cm−1 for CC–H, 2929 cm−1 for C–H of CH3 group, 1733 cm−1 and 1677 cm−1 for CO, 1575 cm−1 and 1384 cm−1 for the bending vibration of CC/N-H and O-H [19], respectively. Wherein, the absorption intensity of stretching vibrations at 1677 cm−1 and 1733 cm−1 of CDs CO dramatic decreases, which is answerable for the formation of hydrogen bonds between CDs and thymine [23], presented as scheme 2 . Consequently, the T-NS-CDs generation could not result in the change of elemental composition and surface bonding states, giving the accordant results and profiles of the XPS analysis compared with that of NS-CDs. As displayed in Fig. 4 , the contents of C, O, N and S are 61.06 (a decrease of 2.69 %), 31.04 (an increase of 6.02 %), 7.32 (an increase of 3.32 %) and 0.58 (a decrease of 0.08 %), respectively.

As we known, CDs are defined as virtually spherical nanomaterials of sizes below 10 nm. Thus, the size and morphology of T-NS-CDs were characterized by TEM. From the TEM observations in Fig. 5
a, it is clear that spherical dots are well-separated with the size range of 4 to 11 nm (an average size of around 6.9 ± 1.19 nm, Fig. 5 b. Moreover, the XRD profile (Fig. 5 c) is shown a broad diffraction peak at 2θ = 26.9°, which would be corresponded to (002) plane for graphitic carbon, suggesting that the formation of amorphous carbon phase [39,40]. Undoubtedly, combining these spectroscopic, the deconvolution analysis of XPS spectra and TEM images, we can speculate that the N, S-co-doped CDs with substantial functional groups on the surface have been prepared successfully, and thymine-functionalization does not drive any aggregation of CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The fluorescent detection for Hg2+ by T-NS-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Ideally, favorable sensor should possess good water solubility, insensitivity to pH, salinity changes and excellent photostability. Thus, these proposed superiorities were investigated in detail based on fluorescence spectrum.

Due to the specific affinity between thymine with Hg2+ [26], T-NS-CDs would be a favorable sensor for Hg2+, which was demonstrated with the help of detailed spectroscopy titration experiments. As can be seen from Fig. 6
a, the titration profiles gradually decrease with the change of Hg2+ concentration in the range of 0–5.1 μM. Correspondingly, in accordance with these spectroscopic changes, the fluorescence color is not almost perceived at the end point of titration (inset of Fig. 6 a). Those observations could be explained by the following facts, one is the electrons transfer from lone pair electrons in N, O, S atoms on the CDs surface to the 6 s or 6p orbitals of Hg2+ [37], giving the nonradioactive recombination [1,43]; and the another one is the formation of specific base pairs (T-Hg2+-T). As a result, the coordination interaction triggered the CDs aggregation, reflecting as pronounced fluorescence quenching [14,44]. Accordingly, the transformation of dispersed CDs into aggregates could be observed in the TEM images (Fig. S10 and Scheme 2).

Then, detailed sensing performance of T-NS-CDs for Hg2+ was inspected. Based on the titration results, the quenching coefficient KSV is found to be 8.09 × 106 M−1 by Stern-Volmer equation. And the fluorescence quenching degree (F0 − F)/F0 at 463 nm is linear to the concentration of Hg2+ with the high correlation coefficient of 0.99, the LOD is also determined to 1.56 nM according to 3σ/S method. Noteworthy, the LOD of T-NS-CDs is much lower than that of CT-CDs (0.47 µM), decreased more than two orders of magnitude, which is the two associated outcome of soft acid–soft base interactions between Hg2+ with the thiol groups [45] and strong T-Hg2+-T coordination [26]. Just as listed in Table 1 , our proposed Hg2+ sensing platform of T-NS-CDs reveals higher sensitivity compared with that of previous reports, manifesting that T-NS-CDs sensing platform has practical application value in aqueous environmental solution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Versatility study of T-NS-CDs for detection of multiple heavy metal ions
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the practical applicability of T-NS-CDs, the versatile detection for multiple heavy metal ions should be considered [46]. As described in Fig. S11 , the Cu2+ and Fe3+-triggered fluorescence quenching of emission intensity at 463 nm is also observed besides Hg2+. It is a windfall gains for us that the thymine-modified T-NS-CDs also could be detected Cu2+ and Fe3+, namely, the amplification of applicability would be realized through the proposed strategy of post-modification. By the way, according to their titration results ( Fig. S12-S13 ), the LODs of Cu2+ and Fe3+ is determined to be 0.77 nM (R2 = 0.99) and 0.76 nM (R2 = 0.99), respectively. In addition, other cations (Na+, K+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Co2+, Ni2+, Cr3+, Zn2+, Mn2+, and Pb2+) brought negligible spectral responses.

The DMSA plays an important role in treatment of heavy metal poisoning such as mercury [47]. Furthermore, DMSA is a strong Hg2+ chelator due to the two S-atom placed in juxta position. Thus, Hg2+ can be removed from the surface of T-NS-CDs by forming Hg-S bond with DMSA (DMSA-Hg2+) [48]. Consequently, the aggregates would be dissociated, resulting in the fluorescence restoration of the T-NS-CDs ( Fig. S14
a). It should be noted that the chelating agent does not show any fluorescence emission ( Fig. S14
b). Detailed sensing ability of T-NS-CDs-Hg2+ ensemble for DMSA was obtained based on the enhanced emission, and the LOD of DMSA was determined to be 1.29 nM ( Fig. S15 ). In fact, the T-Hg2+-T specific base pairs and DMSA-Hg2+ complex stay in states of equilibrium, which is answerable for the incomplete recovery of spectroscopy profile.

Tap water was used to evaluate the practicality and reliability of the method. The tap water was derived from our lab. Firstly, the tap water samples were filtered through 0.22 μm PES water phase membrane. Since no Hg2+ was detected in the samples, and the recovery experiments were performed by spiking Hg2+ into the aqueous samples at concentrations of 50, 70, 90 and 110 μM, respectively. All experiments were performed in triplicate. As shown in Table 2 , the results illustrate that the recoveries for detecting Hg2+ in tap water samples vary in the range of 96.97–101.38 %, and the relative standard deviations (RSD) are less than 2 %, indicating the accuracy of the method.

In summary, N, S co-doped carbon dots (NS-CDs) were prepared through hydrothermal method, which was further modified with thymine (T-NS-CDs) to tailor the surface state and functionalities of CDs. Interestingly, spectroscopic experiments testified that T-NS-CDs have the advantages of water-solubility, monodispersity, excellent photostability, salt stability and tunable luminescence properties. Thus, T-NS-CDs were employed to detect Hg2+ based on the formation of T-Hg2+-T specific base pairs with nanomolar-level detection limit. Furthermore, the interconversion between T-Hg2+-T and DMSA-Hg2+ complex endowed T-NS-CDs with reversible detection of Hg2+. Most importantly, T-NS-CDs also can be applied to the fluorescent sensing for Hg2+ in tap water with satisfactory recoveries, rendering the practicality and reliability of Hg2+ detection method. The present results would provide a new perspective for the design of reusable multi-functional sensors toward interesting targets to achieve reversible detection of different analytes in environmental system.

Min Chen: Data curation, Visualization, Investigation. Jun Wang: Supervision, Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition. Qianbo Zhang: Investigation. Jinsheng Zhang: Formal analysis. Zhiming Chen: Validation. Rongguo Sun: Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (grant No 22161012); The Science and Technology Program of Guizhou Province (grant No ZK[2021]066).
Authors confirm that there are no competing interests to declare.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2022.121998.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Small molecular fluorescent probes for the detection of lead, cadmium and mercury ions
Coord Chem. Rev.

J. Am. Chem. Soc.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Recent advances in carbon quantum dot-based sensing of heavy metals in water, TrAC
Trends Anal. Chem.
